title: Alfajores
subtitle: Argentinian cornflour shortbread sandwiched with dulce de leche
excerpt: Paired discs of an exceptionally tender shortbread made primarily from cornflour, sandwiched with dulce de leche and rolled at the edges in desiccated coconut. Argentina's most widely eaten biscuit. The high proportion of cornflour is what gives the characteristic powdery, melt-in-the-mouth crumb.
sourceNotes: Alfajores have been part of Argentine and Latin American confectionery since the nineteenth century, with roots in a Moorish sweet brought to Spain during the medieval period. The cornflour shortbread version, the Argentine national type, was widespread by the late nineteenth century and is documented in Argentine domestic cookbooks of the early twentieth century. The combination of cornflour-enriched shortbread and dulce de leche filling is consistent across published versions of this period.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze assembled alfajores individually wrapped. Thaw at room temperature for 1 hour.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles straightforwardly. Bake in batches on multiple trays.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Baked biscuit discs keep for three days in a tin. Sandwich and coat on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven. Fan at 140°C. Alfajores should be very pale — barely coloured at the edges, white on top. A few degrees too hot and they colour before the centre is set.

glossaryTerms.definition: The characteristic powdery, melt-in-the-mouth crumb of an alfajor comes from the high proportion of cornflour to plain flour. Cornflour inhibits gluten formation more than plain flour does, giving the biscuit its near-crumbly texture. The dough is fragile and must be chilled before rolling; slight warmth from the hands causes it to crack when cut.

content.text: is the entire point of the biscuit: it should dissolve in the mouth in a way that no standard shortbread does. Keep the dough cold throughout, handle it as little as possible, and pull the biscuits from the oven while they are still very pale. Any colour means overcooking.

Beat the softened butter and icing sugar together until pale. Add the egg yolks and vanilla and beat until combined. Sift in the cornflour, plain flour, baking powder, and salt and mix to a soft dough. Wrap in cling film and refrigerate for 30 minutes. Heat the oven to 160°C conventional. Line two baking trays with baking paper.

Roll the chilled dough on a lightly floured surface to 5 mm thickness. Cut into rounds of 4 to 5 cm diameter and place on the lined trays. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until just set and barely coloured at the edges — the tops remain white. They firm as they cool. Transfer to a rack and cool completely before assembling.

Spread a generous teaspoon of dulce de leche on the flat side of one biscuit, press a second biscuit on top, and gently squeeze until the filling reaches the edges. Roll the exposed edge in desiccated coconut to coat. Repeat with the remaining biscuits. Dust the tops lightly with icing sugar.
